name: Stanley Dance
abstract: Stanley Frank Dance (15 September 1910 in Braintree, Essex – 23 February 1999 in Vista, California) was a British jazz writer, business manager, record producer, and historian of the Swing era. He was personally close to Duke Ellington over a long period, as well as many other musicians; because of this friendship Dance was in a position…

interviews.title: Stanley Dance interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 12, 1998

concerts.label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Louis Armstrong
New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Music of Louis Armstrong
New York Jazz Repertory Company: The Savoy Ballroom "The Home of Happy Feet"

quotes.text: But the records did reasonably well. There was beginners luck in part too, because some, where my heart was really in, didn’t come out so well, but others came out very well. So I go back and these are issued in Europe, with quite good reviews etcetera. And the following year, my wife never really got used to the English climate, and then we were living in a house that was 400 years old you know. And of course the property after World War II in England was at a premium. My father’s house opposite where we lived was 500 years old. But I was accustomed to that. So we decided to move to Connecticut where her father had a place. And he offered us a house sort of rent free. And I thought my wife and I can start up again. For Decca, I had to make I think it was two more albums for Decca. And I’d previously made one with Dickie Wells, the trombone player, and three other trombones, four trombones and a rhythm section, which was unusual but was successful and I made a number of those. And the other one I was counting on to do when Duke’s manager came to me one day and said, “I hear you’re making some more records.” I said yes. “Then why not use my guys — our guys.” I said, “Oh Duke doesn’t want that.” He said, “Yeah, those guys need the money.” So I said, “Well can I have Johnny Hodges?” He says, “Yeah, you can have Johnny Hodges, you can just give him another name.” And so I knew I couldn’t have Duke, you know, so Billy Strayhorn could play piano. And that’s how that album came about, which is rather a fixed subject in — I don’t know whether you’ve read Hasse’s book, well you know it’s completely misinterpreted, it’s as though I was trying to pull a fast one because I knew exactly that Johnny Hodges was a close friend of mine and I knew very well that he was under contract to Norman Granz you know. And then that was the end of Decca and those two. And then in the first batch, one of the things that led to more activity was Earl Hines, another of my idols. He was in San Francisco and I hadn’t the time or the money to go out there, but a Belgian friend of mind was going out and I got him to record a session, which was very successful. And then after a time I had an English friend who was recording, called Dennis Preston, he made quite a few records for him which were issued on English Columbia. And I started to write. I wrote for various jazz magazines — when I had a year when I was the jazz critic of the New York Herald Tribune, which was bad, that was a terrible job really. You’d go to Carnegie Hall and you’d have to leave before © Fillius Jazz Archive -8-
quotes.caption: No mention of Carnegie Hall in the passage
quotes.doc_title: Stanley Dance interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 12, 1998

quotes.text: Well you see that actually worked against him. Because here’s a man who made his name really in 1928. He made “Weather Bird” with Louis and “West End Blues” which are two of the most famous records in jazz history. So people think, would automatically think of him as being old fashioned, because he was of the ‘20s. But he revolutionized piano playing, and he always asked people, other pianists, he said, “How do you do that?” And if they asked him how he did it, he would always show them. And he incorporated new things — the one book, I don’t know, did you read that recent book by Teddy Wilson? It’s worth reading, if you’ve got it in your library there. He tells what makes Earl’s style distinctive, in a way nobody else had. It’s very good. What else did I do? Oh, one other thing. They were having some revival of Basie in George Wein’s thing at Carnegie Hall. I said they should — they had a meeting where we all put up ideas, and I said “well you ought to get back his best singer — Helen Humes.” And for some reason I was in Louisville and I went and spent the night talking to her and practically persuaded her to come back. She had more or less retired. When her mother died she’d been working in a munitions factory and playing bingo, and was very happy. But she came back as a result of that. And she enjoyed — she was another bright talent that’s been forgotten. She was very successful for about ten years I think.
quotes.caption: Recalling Helen Humes' return for a Basie revival
quotes.doc_title: Stanley Dance interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 12, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Nick Brignola
about_quotes.evidence: Stanley Dance is mentioned only as the author of a book ('The World of Duke Ellington by Stanley Dance') which contains a story about the interviewee meeting Harry Carney. The interviewee does not describe a personal relationship with Stanley Dance.
about_quotes.doc_title: Nick Brignola interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, July 28, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: The transcript mentions Stanley Dance only as the author of a book containing an interview with Clarke Terry. The interviewee (Charlie Barnet) does not mention any personal interaction, relationship, or direct knowledge of Stanley Dance himself.

about_quotes.speaker: Dan Morgenstern
about_quotes.evidence: my old friend Stanley Dance, who had by then settled in the U.S. ... We called Earl. We had Stanley Dance help us. Stanley was all for this. ... Stanley later on thought, like some people will with the passing of time, that it had been his idea, which it was not. It was our idea, but we enlisted him

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmie Crawford
